questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with focal seizures. Examination reveals several hypopigmented macules (ash-leaf spots) and a shagreen patch. He has developmental delay. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of hypopigmented macules (ash-leaf spots), shagreen patch, seizures, and developmental delay is classic for tuberous sclerosis. Shagreen patches are specific to tuberous sclerosis; neurofibromatosis presents with café-au-lait spots and Lisch nodules, not ash-leaf spots.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is found to have bilateral renal angiomyolipomas on ultrasound performed for abdominal pain. She has facial reddish papules (angiofibromas) but no neurological symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Hereditary leiomyomatosis with renal cell cancer
questions.explanation: Angiofibromas and bilateral renal angiomyolipomas are pathognomonic for tuberous sclerosis. Birt-Hogg-Dubé presents with fibrofolliculomas and lung cysts, not angiofibromas. Cowden syndrome features trichilemmomas, not angiomyolipomas.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with clusters of flexor spasms and developmental regression. EEG shows hypsarrhythmia. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Tuberous sclerosis is the most common identifiable cause of infantile spasms (West syndrome), often with hypsarrhythmia. Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy may cause spasms but is less common and usually has a clear history. Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy is rare.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known tuberous sclerosis presents with progressive dyspnoea and recurrent pneumothorax. CT chest shows multiple thin-walled cysts. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: LAM is a specific complication of tuberous sclerosis in women, characterised by thin-walled cysts and pneumothorax. Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis also causes cysts but is associated with smoking and has nodular infiltrates. Birt-Hogg-Dubé causes lung cysts but not typically in tuberous sclerosis.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student presents with headache and vomiting. CT brain shows a calcified subependymal nodule and a contrast-enhancing mass at the foramen of Monro causing obstructive hydrocephalus. He has no skin lesions or seizures. What is the single most likely underlying condition?
questions.explanation: The calcified subependymal nodule and mass at the foramen of Monro are characteristic of subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA) in tuberous sclerosis. Neurofibromatosis type 2 presents with schwannomas and meningiomas, not SEGA. von Hippel-Lindau causes hemangioblastomas.
